_source_of_truth_note: This file is the canonical mineral spec for all three runtimes. vugg.py is the dev/test harness (where pytest runs). index.html is the shipped product (GitHub Pages serves repo root; runtime fetches data/minerals.json and overwrites the inline MINERAL_SPEC_FALLBACK). agent-api/vugg-agent.js is intentionally kept simple and reads this file via require(). Cross-runtime drift is caught by tools/sync-spec.js.

_schema.max_nucleation_count: Maximum number of crystals of this species that can ever nucleate in a single run. Counted across the whole simulation (active + dissolved). Keeps common minerals like wulfenite from producing microcrystalline swarms that get immediately swallowed by other phases.
_schema.max_size_cm: Hard cap on crystal size вЂ” 2x world record per research/mineral-accuracy-audit.md. Growth halts when reached; this is the fix for the 321248% bug.
_schema.growth_rate_mult: Relative growth rate, calcite=1.0 baseline. Multiply the runtime base rate.
_schema.T_range_C: [min, max] temperature window for growth (C). null,null = unbounded.
_schema.pH_dissolution_below: pH below which mineral dissolves. null if stable.
_schema.pH_dissolution_above: pH above which mineral dissolves. null if stable.
_schema.required_ingredients: {species: min_ppm}. All must be present for growth.
_schema.thermal_decomp_C: Temperature above which mineral breaks down (C).
_schema.fluorescence: {activator, threshold_ppm, color, quencher} or null if non-fluorescent.
_schema.twin_laws: [{name, miller_indices, trigger, probability}] or [] if none known.
_schema.acid_dissolution: {pH_threshold, reaction, products} or null if acid-stable.
_schema.habit_variants: Array of habit objects. Each has: name, wall_spread (0-1, lateral spread on wall), void_reach (0-1, projection into vug), vector (projecting|coating|tabular|equant|dendritic), trigger (conditions that favor this habit). Growth engine selects habit based on Пѓ, T, and space.
_schema.class: Mineralogical class for filtering/library UI: sulfide | carbonate | oxide | silicate | sulfate | phosphate | hydroxide | halide | arsenate | molybdate | tungstate | native.
_schema.description: 2-3 sentence geological description for library mode. Learning-audience voice; not flavor text.
_schema.scenarios: Array of scenario IDs (from SCENARIOS registry) in which this mineral can nucleate. Empirically verified with seed 42.
_schema.narrate_function: Name of dedicated _narrate_* function. null = inline flavor, needs refactor.
_schema.runtimes_present: Files where growth engine currently exists.
_schema.class_color: Hex color for topographic map visualization. Derived from mineral class вЂ” all classes of the same type share one hue on a 12-color wheel (30В° spacing). Wall/matrix = #D2691E (amber).
_schema.test_cases: Optional array of self-validating test cases for the supersaturation gate. Each case: {name, fluid (partial FluidChemistry), T_C, [pressure_kbar], expects: {sigma: '>1.0' | '==0' | '>=0.5' | '<0.3'}, rationale}. Empty array = no declared cases (legacy minerals). New minerals should declare 2-3 cases minimum: positive gate hit, missing-ingredient block, and (if applicable) a mechanic-specific block. The generic test runner that exercises these cases is item 6 of proposals/TASK-BRIEF-DATA-AS-TRUTH.md (not yet shipped). See proposals/vugg-mineral-template.md В§15 for the schema and tools/new-mineral.py for the scaffold seed.

aragonite.description: CaCOв‚ѓ in the orthorhombic crystal system вЂ” the metastable polymorph of calcite. Same composition, different structure, kinetically favored over thermodynamically-stable calcite when (Folk 1974, Morse 1997, Sun 2015): Mg/Ca > ~1.5 (dominant control вЂ” Mg poisons calcite growth steps but is excluded from aragonite's orthorhombic structure); T > ~50В°C in low-Mg waters; supersaturation О© > ~10 (Ostwald step rule); or trace Sr/Pb/Ba present (these cations match CaВІвЃє in aragonite's 9-fold coordination but not calcite's 6-fold). At surface pressure aragonite is metastable вЂ” over geologic time it converts to calcite (half-life ~10вЃµ yr at 25В°C, ~10Ві yr at 80В°C, faster in hot wet systems).

trace_ingredients.Sr: scavenged into the lattice (aragonite hosts Sr that calcite can't accommodate)
trace_ingredients.Pb: scavenged into the lattice (same reason as Sr)
trace_ingredients.Ba: scavenged into the lattice (same reason as Sr)
trace_ingredients.Mg: EXCLUDED вЂ” Mg's smaller ionic radius doesn't fit aragonite's 9-fold Ca site
trace_ingredients.Mn: minor incorporation (less than calcite, which has the right coordination)
trace_ingredients.Fe: Fe-rich fluids produce the dendritic 'flos ferri' habit

aragonite.thermal_decomp_reaction: orthorhombic CaCO3 -> trigonal calcite (polymorphic conversion before calcination at ~840В°C)

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaCO3 + 2H+ -> Ca2+ + H2O + CO2 (same as calcite вЂ” fizzes)

polymorphic_conversion.trigger: T > 100В°C and aragonite supersaturation drops below 0.8 (kinetic favorability lost)
polymorphic_conversion.half_life_note: Bischoff & Fyfe 1968 вЂ” solution-mediated, ~10Ві yr at 80В°C in water-bearing systems, ~10вЃµ yr at 25В°C, much longer in dry/closed systems. Pleistocene reef aragonite commonly survives; pre-Pleistocene rarely does.

aragonite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: orthorhombic CaCO3 polymorph, Mg/Ca dominant control per Folk 1974/Morse 1997, polymorphic conversion to calcite at T>100В°C, four habits including iconic cyclic twin and flos ferri). Scenarios array empty until SCENARIO-CHEMISTRY-AUDIT brief runs вЂ” none of the existing scenarios have realistic enough Mg/Ca (>1.5) to nucleate aragonite at seed 42; existing scenarios use realistic low-Mg brines that correctly favor calcite. Aragonite gate is verified working with manually elevated Mg.

dolomite.description: CaMg(COв‚ѓ)в‚‚ вЂ” the ordered double carbonate, with Ca and Mg in alternating cation layers (R3М„ space group, distinct from calcite's R3М„c). Rarely forms at surface temperature ('dolomite problem' in geology вЂ” modern oceans should but don't precipitate it for kinetic reasons). Above 50В°C with both Ca and Mg present in roughly comparable amounts (Mg/Ca ratio 0.3-5, optimum 1:1), dolomite competes with calcite (low Mg) and theoretical magnesite (no Ca, not modeled). The host rock of MVT deposits and a major sedimentary carbonate. Saddle-shaped curved rhombohedra are the diagnostic habit вЂ” the lattice strain from cation ordering shows in the surface geometry.
dolomite.thermal_decomp_reaction: CaMg(CO3)2 -> CaO + MgO + 2 CO2 (decarbonation in two stages: MgCO3 first ~500В°C, CaCO3 at ~840В°C)

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaMg(CO3)2 + 4H+ -> Ca2+ + Mg2+ + 2 H2O + 2 CO2 (slower than calcite вЂ” Mg slows the kinetics)

nucleation_preferences.note: Calcite substrate is the classic dolomitization texture вЂ” Mg-bearing fluid replaces earlier calcite as Mg/Ca ratio climbs. Direct vug-wall nucleation also common in Mg-rich brines.

dolomite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: ordered Ca-Mg carbonate, R3М„ space group, three habits including diagnostic saddle rhombs, Mg/Ca ratio gate 0.3-5 with sweet-spot bonus near 1:1). Empirically verified in reactive_wall at seed 42 (initial Mg bumped 80в†’100 within Sverjensky 1981 limestone-brine range). KIM 2023 KINETICS: per Kim, Sun et al. (Science 382:915, doi:10.1126/science.adi3690), dolomite growth requires cyclic dissolution-precipitation. Implementation tracks crystal.phantom_count as cycle counter; growth rate scales by f_ord = 1 - exp(-N/50), gating from 30% (no cycling) to 100% (well-cycled). Steady-state runs produce disordered Mg-calcite intermediate, not true ordered dolomite вЂ” matches the geological observation that most natural settings produce disordered HMC, with true dolomite requiring oscillatory environments (sabkhas, microbial mats, evaporitic basins).

kim_2023_kinetics.citation: Kim J, Kimura Y, Putnis CV, Putnis A, Lee MR, Sun W (2023) Science 382:915-920, doi:10.1126/science.adi3690
kim_2023_kinetics.mechanism: Disordered Ca/Mg surface layer is more soluble than ordered alternation; brief undersaturation strips disordered atoms preferentially, leaving ordered template for next growth pulse
kim_2023_kinetics.cycle_tracking: Fluid-level вЂ” VugConditions._dol_cycle_count counts full undersaturationв†’supersaturation crossings, updated each step in run_step. All active dolomites benefit from accumulated cycles, even those that succeed earlier dolomite seeds enclosed by other carbonates.
kim_2023_kinetics.natural_settings: evaporitic/lacustrine wet-dry cycles (Coorong, Deep Springs Lake), sabkha tidal pumping, microbial mat diurnal pH swings
kim_2023_kinetics.demo_scenario: sabkha_dolomitization (12 flood/evap cycles в†’ ~9 fluid cycles в†’ f_ord ~0.72 в†’ ORDERED dolomite)

siderite.description: FeCOв‚ѓ вЂ” the iron carbonate, a calcite-group mineral (R3М„c) with FeВІвЃє in the Ca site. Tan to deep brown depending on Fe content and trace substitution (manganosiderite в†” rhodochrosite, ankerite в†” dolomite). Hard-gated to REDUCING conditions because FeВІвЃє must stay reduced to be soluble; rising Oв‚‚ progressively converts siderite to goethite/limonite вЂ” the diagenetic 'limonite cube after siderite' pseudomorph mechanism. Forms in MVT veins (accessory), sedimentary ironstone formations, hydrothermal Fe veins, and coal-seam concretions (spherosiderite).

trace_ingredients.Ca: ankerite intermediate (pale brown shift toward dolomite)

siderite.thermal_decomp_reaction: FeCO3 -> Fe3O4/FeO + CO2 (decarbonation; oxidation route at low T -> goethite)

acid_dissolution.reaction: FeCO3 + 2H+ -> Fe2+ + H2O + CO2 (fizzes вЂ” same as calcite)

oxidation_breakdown.note: The textbook diagenetic story. Rising O2 pushes FeВІвЃє в†’ FeВівЃє (insoluble as carbonate); the lattice collapses and Fe + CO3 move on to grow goethite or limonite elsewhere. Mechanism behind 'limonite cube after siderite' pseudomorphs.

nucleation_preferences.note: Pyrite substrate is classic вЂ” Fe-rich sulfide oxidation upstream feeds FeВІвЃє into the carbonate-saturating fluid. In MVT systems, siderite often coats earlier sphalerite + galena as the system cools and CO3 climbs.

siderite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: Fe carbonate, R3М„c calcite-group structure, four habits including spherosiderite, oxidation breakdown to goethite/limonite вЂ” the diagenetic pseudomorph mechanism). Empirically verified in mvt at seed 42.

rhodochrosite.description: MnCOв‚ѓ вЂ” the rosy manganese carbonate, the pink mineral of cabinet specimens. Same R3М„c calcite-group structure with MnВІвЃє replacing CaВІвЃє. Forms a continuous solid solution toward calcite via the kutnohorite (CaMn carbonate) intermediate, producing the diagnostic agate-like banding when Mn/Ca ratios oscillate. Color is intrinsic (MnВІвЃє chromophore), not a trace activator. Stability requires moderate-to-reducing conditions: aggressive oxidation flips MnВІвЃє to MnВівЃє/MnвЃґвЃє and the surface converts to black manganese oxides (pyrolusite, psilomelane). Forms in epithermal Mn-bearing veins (Capillitas Argentina, Sweet Home Colorado), metamorphosed Mn sediments (N'Chwaning South Africa), and low-T carbonate replacement zones.

trace_ingredients.Ca: kutnohorite intermediate solid solution (Ca-rich pales the color; Mn-dominant gives deep raspberry-red)
trace_ingredients.Fe: darkens toward brown when Fe-rich (siderite intermediate)

rhodochrosite.thermal_decomp_reaction: MnCO3 -> MnO + CO2 (calcination, lower than calcite's 840В°C)

fluorescence.note: MnВІвЃє is the intrinsic chromophore; the same ion is the activator in calcite/sphalerite. Some rhodochrosite fluoresces dull red under LW UV, but the high Mn content can be self-quenching.

acid_dissolution.reaction: MnCO3 + 2H+ -> Mn2+ + H2O + CO2 (fizzes вЂ” same as calcite)

oxidation_breakdown.reaction: MnCO3 + O2 -> Mn3+/Mn4+ oxide rind (pyrolusite/psilomelane staining) + CO2
oxidation_breakdown.note: MnВІвЃє is the cation that makes rhodochrosite pink; oxidation converts it to higher valence Mn that forms black insoluble oxides. Museum specimens require careful storage to avoid surface darkening.

nucleation_preferences.note: Goethite substrate produces the Capillitas-style stalactitic habit. Sulfide substrate (pyrite, sphalerite, galena) is the classic epithermal vein paragenesis вЂ” rhodochrosite fills space between earlier sulfides as the system cools.

rhodochrosite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: Mn carbonate, R3М„c calcite-group structure, four habits, Ca substitution в†’ kutnohorite intermediate color shift, oxidation breakdown to Mn-oxide rind). Empirically verified in mvt at seed 42 (initial Mn bumped 8в†’25 within Tri-State district brine range per Roedder 1976). The biggest collection-weight miss is now in the game (35 specimens). Future SCENARIO-CHEMISTRY-AUDIT will surface it more broadly via dedicated Capillitas / Sweet Home / N'Chwaning scenarios.

wurtzite.description: (Zn,Fe)S, the hexagonal high-temperature dimorph of sphalerite вЂ” same composition, different crystal structure. Above 95В°C, cubic sphalerite gives way to hexagonal wurtzite with the distinctive hemimorphic habit (one end pointed, one end flat, due to the non-centrosymmetric P6в‚ѓmc space group). On cooling below 95В°C, wurtzite converts to sphalerite вЂ” but sphalerite rarely goes back. The asymmetric dimorphism means finding wurtzite is diagnostic of rapid cooling from the hexagonal field.

trace_ingredients.Fe: substitutes for Zn, darkens color (same as sphalerite, but hotter so more Fe uptake)

wurtzite.thermal_decomp_reaction: hexagonal ZnS в†’ sublimation (shares sphalerite decomposition)

acid_dissolution.reaction: (Zn,Fe)S + 2H+ в†’ Zn2+ + H2S (slow, only in strong acid вЂ” same as sphalerite)

wurtzite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: T>95 sphalerite dimorph, hexagonal P6в‚ѓmc, hemimorphic habit, polymorphic inversion handled).

pyrite.description: FeSв‚‚, fool's gold. Enormous T range (25-700В°C), always reducing. Oxidation produces goethite/limonite plus sulfuric acid вЂ” the acid-mine-drainage reaction. Three common habits: cubic with striated faces, 12-face pyritohedral, and raspberry-shaped framboidal aggregates from sedimentary microbial sulfate reduction.

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative dissolution вЂ” pyrite + O2 + H2O -> Fe2+ + SO4 + H+ (acid mine drainage)

marcasite.description: FeSв‚‚, the orthorhombic dimorph of pyrite вЂ” same formula, different crystal structure. Acidic (pH<5), low-T (<240В°C) fluids switch FeSв‚‚ from cubic pyrite to orthorhombic marcasite. Diagnostic cockscomb and spearhead habits, paler brass than pyrite with iridescent tarnish. Metastable: converts to pyrite over geologic time or on heating, and rots in humid air to sulfuric acid and iron sulfate (the classic museum-specimen problem).
marcasite.thermal_decomp_reaction: orthorhombic FeS2 в†’ cubic FeS2 (pyrite) вЂ” metastable inversion, not true decomposition

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative breakdown in humid air вЂ” FeS2 + O2 + H2O в†’ FeSO4 + H2SO4 (museum rot); extreme acid dissolves directly

nucleation_preferences.note: Acid-loving FeS2 dimorph. In MVT deposits marcasite often coats earlier sphalerite/galena. Subsurface goethite/limonite pseudomorphs after marcasite are the classic Egyptian Prophecy Stones.

marcasite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: pH<5 & T<240 dimorph of pyrite, full habit set, metastable inversion handled).

jarosite.description: KFeВівЃєв‚ѓ(SOв‚„)в‚‚(OH)в‚†, the diagnostic acid-mine-drainage mineral. Yellow-to-ocher pseudocubic rhombs and powdery crusts; the supergene Fe-sulfate that takes over from goethite when pH drops below 4. Confirmed on Mars at Meridiani Planum by NASA's MER Opportunity MГ¶ssbauer spectrometer in 2004 (KlingelhГ¶fer et al. 2004) вЂ” the first confirmation of past liquid water + acidic conditions on Mars. alunite.description: KAlв‚ѓ(SOв‚„)в‚‚(OH)в‚†, the K-Al sister of jarosite (alunite group, isostructural). brochantite.description: Cuв‚„(SOв‚„)(OH)в‚†, the wet-supergene Cu sulfate. Emerald-green prismatic crystals, distinguishable from malachite by distinctly darker green and prismatic habit (vs malachite's botryoidal). The higher-pH end (pH 4-7) of the brochantite в†” antlerite Cu-sulfate fork вЂ” acidification (pH < 3) converts brochantite to antlerite + Hв‚‚O; alkalinization (pH > 7) converts to tenorite/malachite. Atacama Desert (Chile) supergene Cu deposits вЂ” Chuquicamata, Mansa Mina, Mantos Blancos, El Tesoro вЂ” have brochantite as a major component. Bisbee, Mt Lyell (Tasmania), Cap Garonne (France), Cananea (Mexico) all have well-known specimens. Statue of Liberty's iconic green patina is largely brochantite (chloride-rich harbor air drives the SOв‚„/COв‚ѓ partition toward sulfate, not malachite).

anhydrite.description: CaSOв‚„, the anhydrous calcium sulfate; sister of selenite/gypsum (CaSOв‚„В·2Hв‚‚O). The anhydrite-gypsum boundary is not a single line but an XY plot of T vs salinity (Hardie 1967): in pure water anhydrite stable above 60В°C, but in saline brines (>100вЂ° NaCl-eq) anhydrite stable down to surface T due to lowered water activity. Two distinct geological occurrences: (1) high-T hydrothermal (porphyry-Cu deep-brine zones at 200-700В°C вЂ” Bingham Roedder 1971); (2) low-T evaporite (Persian Gulf / Coorong sabkha + Salar de Atacama вЂ” anhydrite + halite + dolomite stratigraphy). Below 60В°C in dilute fluid, anhydrite is metastable and rehydrates to gypsum/selenite. Naica's Cave of Crystals shows exactly this: an older anhydrite floor preserved from the deeper brine phase, overgrown by giant selenite blades when the cave dewatered + cooled. Industrial: cement-setting accelerator, sulfur source, plaster manufacture (anhydrite в†” gypsum hydration cycle). Pale-lavender 'angelite' Peruvian variety is a metaphysical-stone fixture.

acid_dissolution.reaction: rehydrates to gypsum (CaSO4В·2H2O = selenite in sim) at T<60В°C AND salinity<100вЂ°; releases CaВІвЃє + SOв‚„ВІвЃ» to feed selenite re-precipitation
acid_dissolution.note: Not acid-dissolution вЂ” REHYDRATION to gypsum. The anhydrite в†” gypsum cycle is the diagnostic Naica paragenesis. Hysteresis at salinity<95вЂ° to avoid oscillation.

bornite.description: Cuв‚…FeSв‚„ вЂ” the 228В°C order-disorder transition mineral. Above 228В°C, Cu and Fe randomly occupy the cation sites (pseudo-cubic, high-T form); below, they order (orthorhombic). Fresh bronze-copper color on a broken surface, but the mineral tarnishes rapidly to the iridescent blue/purple/green/gold 'peacock ore' from thin-film interference on sulfide surface products. Forms both hypogene (hydrothermal, ~200вЂ“500В°C) and supergene (cooler, as an enrichment mineral replacing chalcopyrite). Competes with chalcopyrite for Cu+Fe+S at different Cu:Fe ratios.

native_gold.description: Au вЂ” elemental native gold, the noble metal. Two precipitation pathways the sim collapses into one supersaturation function: (1) high-T magmatic-hydrothermal Au-Cl complex destabilization at boiling/decompression (Bingham vapor-plume mechanism per Landtwing et al. 2010), and (2) low-T supergene Au-Cl reduction at the redox interface, often coupled with chalcocite enrichment (Bisbee oxidation-cap mechanism per Graeme et al. 2019). Tolerates both oxidizing AND reducing fluids because the two transport complexes (Au-Cl and Au-HS) cover both Eh regimes. Crystal habits range from octahedral {111} (slow growth, rare) through dendritic / spongy (the diagnostic supergene Au) to massive nuggets (rapid precipitation in pockets). Color goes from rich yellow at pure Au through pale yellow electrum (>10% Ag alloy) to rose-gold cuproauride affinity (Cu-rich systems like Bisbee). Essentially indestructible in surface conditions вЂ” no acid-dissolution path in the engine.

trace_ingredients.Te: Au-Te telluride competition (calaverite/sylvanite if Te + S available вЂ” future engine)

native_gold.thermal_decomp_reaction: Au melts at 1064В°C; mineral itself stable at all sim T (no thermal decomposition path)

lepidocrocite.description: Оі-FeOOH вЂ” the ruby-red hydroxide dimorph of goethite. Same formula, different crystal structure: goethite is a 3D framework (yellow-brown needles), lepidocrocite is layered (ruby-red micaceous plates). Particle size controls the hue вЂ” nanoscale plates appear pink to mauve, coarser crystals rust-red. Kinetically favored over goethite under RAPID oxidation of FeВІвЃє (e.g., pyrite weathering in situ); slow oxidation gives goethite instead. Transforms to goethite over geological time or to maghemite/hematite when heated. 'Lithium quartz' sold in rock shops is actually nanoscale lepidocrocite included in quartz.

acanthite.description: Agв‚‚S вЂ” monoclinic silver sulfide and the most important silver ore on Earth at 87% Ag by weight. Lead-gray to iron-black metallic, Mohs 2-2.5, sectile (cuts like lead). native_silver.description: Ag вЂ” elemental silver, the only native element bright enough to make you rich just by looking at it wrong. Cubic isometric (Fm3М„m), Mohs 2.5-3, specific gravity 10.5 (one of the heaviest native metals). The S-depletion mineral: native silver only forms where every sulfur atom is already claimed and the fluid is strongly reducing вЂ” the inverse of normal supersaturation logic. Famous wire-silver localities (Kongsberg Norway, Keweenaw Michigan, Cobalt Ontario) all sit in S-depleted reducing pockets. Tarnishes inevitably to a dark acanthite rind from atmospheric Hв‚‚S; freshly broken surfaces still flash with the brightest metallic luster known to mineralogy. cobaltite.description: CoAsS вЂ” orthorhombic cobalt sulfarsenide (pseudo-cubic, pyritohedral habit). Tin-white with diagnostic pinkish blush from surface Co oxidation, Mohs 5.5. The cobalt analog of arsenopyrite. The three-element gate (Co + As + S all required simultaneously) makes this rare; the Cobalt-Ontario type locality is one of the few places it forms in well-developed crystals. Indian jewellers used powdered cobaltite ('sehta') to make blue enamel on gold ornaments вЂ” the source of the first Co ever isolated from a non-arsenide mineral. Sources: research/research-cobaltite.md (boss commit f2939da), Bayliss 1968 (Mineral. Mag. 36, on cobaltite-arsenopyrite substitution).
cobaltite.audit_status: Round 8c-4 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. THREE-ELEMENT GATE engine: Co + As + S all required simultaneously at minimum thresholds (Coв‰Ґ50, Asв‰Ґ100, Sв‰Ґ50). First three-element gate in the sim. Reducing (Oв‚‚<0.5) + T 300-600В°C with peak 400-500. Substrate preference: arsenopyrite (the paragenetic predecessor). Habit variants: massive_granular (low Пѓ, ore form), pyritohedral (mid Пѓ, {210} striated faces вЂ” Cobalt Ontario diagnostic), reniform (high Пѓ, botryoidal). Glaucodot series captured in narrator: Fe>100 in fluid yields '(Co,Fe)AsS' note вЂ” the iron-rich end of the cobaltite-glaucodot solid solution (Skutterud type). Engine includes oxidative-dissolution branch: when Oв‚‚>0.7, releases CoВІвЃє + AsOв‚„ВівЃ» + SOв‚„ВІвЃ» to fluid, feeding the existing erythrite engine downstream вЂ” this is THE primary source for the magenta-pink 'cobalt bloom' supergene cascade. Activates with Round 8c-1 Bisbee Co=80 + As=8 + S=150 chemistry; expected to fire in seed-42 Bisbee primary stage. Sources: Bayliss 1968 (cobaltite-arsenopyrite substitution), boss research file research/research-cobaltite.md.

chalcanthite.description: CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O вЂ” the bright sky-blue water-soluble Cu sulfate, the [Cu(Hв‚‚O)в‚…]ВІвЃє chromophore one of the most intensely-colored minerals. Triclinic (P1М„), Mohs 2.5, perfect {110} cleavage. The most water-soluble mineral in the sim вЂ” re-dissolves when fluid salinity drops below 4 OR pH rises above 5 (per-step hook in VugSimulator.run_step). Geologically authentic: every chalcanthite specimen is a temporary victory over entropy. The terminal phase of the Cu-sulfate oxidation cascade (chalcopyrite в†’ bornite в†’ chalcocite в†’ covellite в†’ cuprite в†’ brochantite в†’ antlerite в†’ chalcanthite). Forms in arid strongly-oxidizing very-acidic salt-concentrated drainage: Chuquicamata mine walls (Chile), Rio Tinto AMD seeps (Spain), Atacama desert evaporite crusts. Most 'natural' specimens on the market are lab-grown. Sources: research/research-chalcanthite.md (boss commit f2939da), Bandy 1938 (Am. Mineral. 23).

rosasite.description: (Cu,Zn)в‚‚(COв‚ѓ)(OH)в‚‚ вЂ” monoclinic supergene carbonate, the Cu-dominant member of the rosasite-aurichalcite pair. Velvety blue-green botryoidal spheres on weathered Cu-Zn sulfide deposits; the diagnostic habit is sky-blue spherical aggregates on red limonite (Mapimi, Mexico). Forms when chalcopyrite + sphalerite weather together at the supergene front, releasing CuВІвЃє + ZnВІвЃє + COв‚ѓВІвЃ» into shallow groundwater that precipitates the carbonate at near-neutral pH. The Cu:Zn ratio in the parent fluid is the key control: rosasite forms when Cu/(Cu+Zn) > 0.5; aurichalcite forms when the ratio inverts вЂ” same broth, different outcome. Mohs 4, blue-green streak, splintery fracture. Type locality: Rosas Mine, Sardinia (1908). Source: Pinch & Wilson 1977 (Tsumeb monograph); research/research-rosasite.md.

torbernite.description: Cu(UOв‚‚)в‚‚(POв‚„)в‚‚В·12Hв‚‚O вЂ” tetragonal uranyl phosphate, the P-branch of the autunite-group anion-competition trio (with zeunerite for AsOв‚„ВівЃ» and carnotite for VOв‚„ВівЃ» the other branches). Forms emerald-green to apple-green thin tabular plates that look like green mica; strongly radioactive (UвЃ¶вЃє in the lattice), notably non-fluorescent (CuВІвЃє quenches uranyl emission). Forms at the supergene oxidation front when uraninite weathers to mobile UвЃ¶вЃє and meets phosphate-bearing groundwater alongside CuВІвЃє вЂ” torbernite wins the cation race when P/(P+As) > 0.5 in the local fluid. Mohs 2-2.5, dehydrates irreversibly to metatorbernite (8 Hв‚‚O) above ~75В°C. Type locality: Schneeberg, Saxony (Ore Mountains). Sources: Pinch & Wilson 1977 monographs; research/research-torbernite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a).

zeunerite.description: Cu(UOв‚‚)в‚‚(AsOв‚„)в‚‚В·(10-16)Hв‚‚O вЂ” tetragonal uranyl arsenate, the As-branch of the autunite-group anion-competition trio. Visually almost indistinguishable from torbernite вЂ” same emerald-green color, same square tabular habit, same micaceous cleavage вЂ” distinguishable only by chemistry. The arsenic is the giveaway: zeunerite localities are almost always former mining districts with arsenic-bearing primary ores (arsenopyrite, tennantite). Forms at the supergene oxidation front when uraninite weathers alongside As-rich primary sulfides; zeunerite wins the anion race when As/(P+As) > 0.5. Mohs 2.5; dehydrates irreversibly to metazeunerite (8 Hв‚‚O) above ~75В°C; non-fluorescent (CuВІвЃє quenches). Type locality: Schneeberg, Saxony (1872). Sources: Pinch & Wilson 1977; research/research-zeunerite.md.

carnotite.description: Kв‚‚(UOв‚‚)в‚‚(VOв‚„)в‚‚В·3Hв‚‚O вЂ” monoclinic uranyl vanadate, the V-branch of the autunite-group anion-competition trio (with torbernite for phosphate and zeunerite for arsenate). Bright canary-yellow earthy crusts on sandstone вЂ” the mineral that paints the desert. One percent of carnotite by weight is enough to stain an entire Jurassic outcrop the color of school buses, which is how the Colorado Plateau uranium districts were prospected before instruments. Formed where oxidizing groundwater carries UвЃ¶вЃє + VвЃµвЃє + KвЃє together at the supergene front, often concentrated around organic reducing traps (petrified wood, carbonaceous shales). Mohs ~2 (soft, earthy), strongly radioactive, non-fluorescent (vanadate matrix quenches the uranyl emission). Type locality: Roc Creek, Montrose County, Colorado (1899). Sources: research/research-carnotite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a); Colorado Plateau uranium-vanadium roll-front geology.

carnotite.audit_status: Round 9c (Apr 2026), updated 9e (May 2026). V-branch of the uranyl anion-competition trio (with torbernite for P-branch, zeunerite for As-branch, both shipped in 9b). 9e added a K/(K+Ca) > 0.5 cation gate to fork against tyuyamunite (Ca-cation analog). Different cation (K instead of Cu), different crystal system (monoclinic instead of tetragonal), different habit (canary-yellow earthy crusts instead of emerald-green tabular plates), same anion-competition mechanic. supersaturation_carnotite returns 0 when V/(P+As+V) < 0.5 OR when K/(K+Ca) < 0.5. No shipped scenario has the K+U+V+low-T+oxidizing conjunction with K-dominant Ca, so carnotite + tyuyamunite are both content-gap minerals (same status as 9b/9d). Engine + test_cases verified. Sources: research/research-carnotite.md + research/research-tyuyamunite.md; Colorado Plateau roll-front geology; Roc Creek type locality (1899).

autunite.description: Ca(UOв‚‚)в‚‚(POв‚„)в‚‚В·11Hв‚‚O вЂ” tetragonal uranyl phosphate, the Ca-cation analog of torbernite. Bright canary-yellow tabular plates on the same {001} habit, but where torbernite's CuВІвЃє quenches the uranyl emission, autunite's CaВІвЃє does not вЂ” under longwave UV (365nm) it glows intense apple-green, one of the brightest fluorescent species known. Round 9d cation-fork mechanic: same parent fluid as torbernite (U + P + supergene-T + oxidizing), differentiated by Cu/(Cu+Ca) ratio. Ca dominates groundwater everywhere except in actively-mined Cu districts, so autunite is far more common in nature than torbernite (which gets all the museum attention). Mohs 2-2.5; dehydrates irreversibly to meta-autunite (8 Hв‚‚O) above ~80В°C. Type locality: Saint-Symphorien, Autun, France (Adrien Brongniart, 1852). Sources: Pinch & Wilson 1977; research/research-uraninite.md В§164-178 (paragenetic chain).

fluorescence.status: the brightest uranyl fluorescer in the simulator вЂ” CaВІвЃє doesn't quench like CuВІвЃє does in torbernite/zeunerite. LW UV (365nm) drives the strongest emission; SW (254nm) gives a duller green-yellow.

autunite.audit_status: Round 9d (May 2026): Ca-cation analog of torbernite, completing the cation-fork generalization on the P-branch of the uranyl-anion-competition mechanic (9b/9c was the anion fork P-vs-As-vs-V; 9d is the cation fork Cu-vs-Ca on the P-branch alone). supersaturation_autunite returns 0 when Ca/(Cu+Ca) < 0.5 OR when P/(P+As+V) < 0.5. supersaturation_torbernite gains a symmetric Cu/(Cu+Ca) > 0.5 gate (was Cu>=5 only). Autunite is the geologically-default uranyl phosphate (Ca >>> Cu in groundwater); torbernite is the special case (Cu-dominant fluid in mining districts). Habit variants mirror torbernite (tabular_plates/micaceous_book/encrusting). Source: research/research-uraninite.md В§Variants for Game В§4 (boss canonical 626bb22, May 2026).

tyuyamunite.description: Ca(UOв‚‚)в‚‚(VOв‚„)в‚‚В·5-8Hв‚‚O вЂ” orthorhombic Ca-uranyl vanadate, the cation analog of carnotite. Forms by the same mechanism (oxidizing groundwater + uraninite weathering + V-bearing fluid) but wins when Ca/(K+Ca) > 0.5 вЂ” the geological-default cation in sandstone groundwater. The two species are commonly intergrown in Colorado Plateau and Tyuya-Muyun deposits, with Britannica noting they are interconvertible by cation exchange. Canary-yellow earthy crusts staining sandstone, weakly-to-moderately fluorescent yellow-green LW UV (vanadate matrix dampens emission slightly even with Ca-cation; cleaner than carnotite's yellow but not as bright as autunite/uranospinite). Mohs 1.5-2, dehydrates readily to metatyuyamunite (В·3-5Hв‚‚O) at room T in dry storage. Type locality: Tyuya-Muyun, Fergana Valley, Kyrgyzstan (Nenadkevich 1912). Source: research/research-tyuyamunite.md (implementation-grade draft, 2026-05-01); cation-fork mechanic mirrors carnotite + the Round 9d torbernite/autunite pair.

uranospinite.description: Ca(UOв‚‚)в‚‚(AsOв‚„)в‚‚В·10Hв‚‚O вЂ” tetragonal Ca-uranyl arsenate, the autunite-group cation analog of zeunerite. Same parent fluid as zeunerite (U + As + supergene-T + oxidizing) but wins when Ca/(Cu+Ca) > 0.5 вЂ” typically when Cu has been depleted from the local fluid but As is still around. Strongly fluorescent yellow-green LW UV вЂ” CaВІвЃє doesn't quench like CuВІвЃє does in zeunerite, putting uranospinite in the same 'bright fluorescer' tier as autunite (slightly dampened by the heavier As-anion vibrational coupling). Tabular yellow plates that look like a yellow autunite, with the As content as the chemistry giveaway. Mohs 2-3, dehydrates readily to metauranospinite. Type locality: Walpurgis Flacher vein, Weisser Hirsch Mine, Schneeberg, Saxony (Weisbach 1873). Sources: research/research-uranospinite.md (implementation-grade draft, 2026-05-01); MSA Handbook of Mineralogy.
